Survival guide
Survival Guide
Map
Settings
Survival Guide
Choose a section:
Back
Firecraft
Collect dry tinder and kindling first, then build a stable fire lay before lighting.
Fire setup photo 1
Fire setup photo 2
Firecraft & Warmth
Firecraft and warmth basics.
Firecraft image placeholder 1
Firecraft image placeholder 2
The Teepee Fire (Quick Heat)
Image placeholder: Twigs in a cone shape over dry tinder in the center.
Place a handful of dry tinder (grass, bark) in the center of your cleared fire ring.
Lean small, dry twigs over the tinder in a circular cone (teepee) shape. Leave a small gap to reach the tinder.
Light the tinder from the bottom. As the teepee burns and collapses, add thicker logs to the pile.
The Log Cabin Fire (Long Burn)
Image placeholder: Crisscross stacked sticks around a small center teepee.
Build a tiny, basic Teepee fire in the center.
Place two thick branches parallel to each other on opposite sides of the teepee.
Place two smaller branches across them perpendicularly. Continue stacking upward for 4 to 5 levels, then light the center teepee.
The Lean-To Fire (For Wind/Rain)
Image placeholder: Large log on the ground with small sticks leaning over tinder underneath.
Find a very thick, slightly damp log and place it horizontally, perpendicular to the direction the wind is blowing.
Place your dry tinder bundle right up against the bottom of this large log on the downwind side.
Lean smaller, dry sticks over the tinder, resting them on top of the large log. Light the tinder; the large log blocks the wind and reflects heat outward.
Striking a Ferro Rod
Image placeholder: Scraper at a 45-degree angle on a ferro rod with sparks falling into tinder.
Anchor the tip of the Ferro rod firmly directly into the center of your dry tinder bundle.
Place the metal scraper at a 45-degree angle at the top of the rod.
Instead of pushing the scraper down (which smashes your tinder), pull the rod firmly backward to drop a shower of 3,000-degree sparks into the tinder.
Finding Dry Tinder in the Wet
Image placeholder: Knife carving a feather stick with curled shavings still attached.
Never take wood from the ground. Break off dead branches that are still attached to standing trees.
Use your knife to carve away the wet, outer bark until you reach the pale, dry wood underneath.
Carefully shave the dry wood downward to create thin, curly shavings that stay attached to the stick (Feather Sticks). These will catch a spark even if the forest is soaked.
Water
Find flowing water when possible and purify it by boiling, filtering, or chemical treatment.
Water source photo 1
Water source photo 2
Water Procurement
Water procurement basics.
Water procurement image placeholder 1
Water procurement image placeholder 2
The Transpiration Bag
Image placeholder: Clear plastic bag tied over a leafy branch with condensed water pooling at the corner.
Find a living, broad-leaved tree or bush directly in the sunlight (avoid poisonous plants).
Place a clear plastic bag over a densely leaved branch and tie the open end tightly shut around the branch.
Weigh down the bottom corner of the bag with a small pebble so the condensed water pools in one spot. Check back in a few hours.
The Cloth Dew Sponge
Image placeholder: Cloth tied around ankles while walking through tall wet morning grass.
Wake up before the sun evaporates the morning dew.
Tie a clean, highly absorbent cloth (like a cotton t-shirt or bandana) around your ankles and walk through tall grass.
Once the cloth is completely soaked, wring the collected water directly into your container. This water is generally safe to drink without boiling.
Natural Layer Filter
Image placeholder: Inverted bottle filter with layers of gravel, sand, charcoal, and cloth, dripping cleaner water.
Create a funnel (e.g., cut the bottom off a plastic bottle, or use bark/cloth). Poke a small hole in the bottom.
Pack the funnel in layers from bottom to top: Cloth/grass, crushed charcoal (from your fire), fine sand, and small gravel at the top.
Pour murky water slowly through the top. Crucial: This only removes dirt and toxins; it does NOT kill bacteria. You must still boil this water.
Safe Boiling Rules
Image placeholder: Metal canteen over a campfire at a full rolling boil.
Filter your water first to remove floating debris and dirt (see Natural Layer Filter).
Place your metal container in or suspended directly over the fire.
Bring the water to a rapid, rolling boil (large bubbles breaking the surface) and keep it boiling for at least 1 full minute (or 3 minutes if you are at a high altitude). Let cool before drinking.
Tracking Hidden Water
Image placeholder: V-shaped deer tracks in muddy ground leading downhill into a ravine.
Follow the animals. Grazing animals (like deer) usually head toward water at dawn and dusk. Follow their tracks downhill.
Look for insects. Bees rarely fly more than 3-4 miles from their hives, and ants will often march in a straight line toward moisture.
Dig in dry riverbeds. Find the lowest point on the outside edge of a bend in a dry stream and dig down a foot; water will often seep into the hole.
Emergency Shelter
Emergency shelter basics.
Emergency shelter image placeholder 1
Emergency shelter image placeholder 2
Choosing a Safe Location
Image placeholder: Flat high ground away from widowmakers and low riverbanks.
Avoid the 4 W\'s: Wind (exposed ridges), Water (dry riverbeds that can flash flood), Widowmakers (dead branches hanging above), and Wildlife (game trails or nests).
Look for flat, slightly elevated ground that drains well if it rains.
Clear away rocks, sharp twigs, and debris to create a smooth, flat surface before you start building.
Ground Insulation (The Bed)
Image placeholder: Person on thick pine-bough bedding above cold ground inside shelter.
Never sleep directly on bare dirt or rock. The cold ground will conduct heat away from your body faster than the cold air.
Gather dry materials like pine boughs, dry grass, or piles of dead leaves.
Build a natural mattress that is at least 6 to 8 inches thick after it is compressed by your body weight.
The Basic Lean-To
Image placeholder: Ridgepole between trees with angled ribs facing a small fire.
Find two trees about 6 feet apart. Lash a thick, sturdy branch (the ridgepole) horizontally between them at chest height.
Lean smaller branches (ribs) closely together against the downwind side of the ridgepole at a 45-degree angle.
Weave smaller twigs horizontally through the ribs, then pile on a thick layer of leaves or pine needles to block wind and rain.
The Debris Hut (Cold Weather)
Image placeholder: Wedge-shaped debris hut with a long ridgepole and heavy leaf insulation.
Prop one end of a strong, 8-foot pole onto a low stump or rock (about waist high). Let the other end rest on the ground.
Lean short sticks tightly along both sides of the pole to create a small A-frame. It should be just big enough for you to slide into.
Pile at least 2 to 3 feet of dry leaves, moss, or pine needles over the entire outside of the structure to act as heavy insulation.
The A-Frame Tarp Shelter
Image placeholder: Tarp draped over a ridgeline and staked tight into a triangular tent.
Tie a rope or piece of paracord tightly between two trees at about waist or chest height.
Drape your emergency blanket or tarp evenly over the line.
Pull the four corners out tightly to form a triangle and peg them into the ground using sharpened sticks or heavy rocks.
First Aid
Address major bleeding first, clean wounds, and monitor for shock while seeking help.
First aid photo 1
First aid photo 2
Signaling
Use clear visual or sound signals in open areas and repeat patterns to attract attention.
Signaling photo 1
Signaling photo 2
